\Title{A Comparative Evaluation of SPARQ Against Leading Metaheuristics}

\TitleCitation{A Comparative Evaluation of SPARQ Against Leading Metaheuristics}

\Author{Vasileios Charilogis$^{1}$, Ioannis G. Tsoulos$^{2,*}$ and Anna
Maria Gianni$^{3}$}

\AuthorNames{Vasileios Charilogis, Ioannis G. Tsoulos and Anna Maria Gianni.}

\AuthorCitation{Charilogis, V., Tsoulos, I.G., Gianni, A. M.}


\address{$^{1}$\quad{}Department of Informatics and Telecommunications,
University of Ioannina, 47150 Kostaki Artas, Greece, v.charilog@uoi.gr\\
$^{2}$\quad{}Department of Informatics and Telecommunications, University
of Ioannina, 47150 Kostaki Artas, Greece, itsoulos@uoi.gr\\
$^{3}$\quad{}Department of Informatics and Telecommunications, University
of Ioannina, 47150 Kostaki Artas, Greece, am.gianni@uoi.gr}


\corres{Correspondence: itsoulos@uoi.gr}


\abstract{SPARQ is an advanced global optimization algorithm, the direct evolution
of an earlier method called ARQ2. It searches efficiently for the
best solution to problems where the space of options is too vast to
check exhaustively, such as tuning antenna arrays or planning fuel-efficient
spacecraft trajectories. Like its predecessor, it works with a population
of candidate solutions that improve generation after generation, alternating
between two complementary search strategies. What sets SPARQ apart
is that it makes nearly every part of this process adaptive. Its population
shrinks intelligently as the search matures. Its internal settings
draw from a memory of many past successful configurations, not a single
average. Its escape-from-stagnation mechanisms come in graduated strength,
from a gentle nudge to a deeper partial restart. It also adds capabilities
its predecessor never had, including a dedicated phase that locally
polishes the current best solution using its own memory of productive
directions. Every addition is kept only where it has been proven to
genuinely help. The result is markedly more reliable than its predecessor,
while staying grounded in the same battle-tested core.}


\keyword{SPARQ; Continuous optimization; Evolutionary algorithm; Adaptive
parameter control; Metaheuristics; Differential evolution; Algorithm
benchmarking; Population-based search;}

\section{Introduction}

Optimization problems in which the goal is to locate the best possible
configuration of a set of continuous variables, without relying on
gradient information and without any guarantee that the objective
function is smooth, convex, or even continuous, arise throughout engineering,
physics, and applied economics. Formally, such problems are instances
of global optimization:

\[
\min_{\mathbf{x}\in\Omega}f(\mathbf{x}),\qquad\Omega:=[\ell_{1},u_{1}]\times\dots\times[\ell_{D},u_{D}]\subset\mathbb{R}^{D},
\]

where $f:\Omega\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$ is accessed only through pointwise
evaluations, and derivative information may be unavailable. Given
an evaluation budget $N_{fe}$\LyXZeroWidthSpace , an algorithm produces
a sequence of iterates $\{\mathbf{x}_{t}\}_{t=1}^{N_{fe}}$ and tracks
the best-so-far value

\[
f_{\min}(t):=\min_{1\le s\le t}f(\mathbf{x}_{s})
\]

The task is to locate the global minimum (or maximum) of $f$ over
$\Omega$ despite the presence of numerous local optima, ill-conditioning,
and, in many real applications, a strict limit $N_{fe}$ on how many
times the objective function can be evaluated. The difficulty of this
task grows sharply with the dimension $D$, and no single search strategy
can be expected to perform best on every possible problem instance
\citep{problem}.

In response, the optimization literature has developed several broad
families of methods. Exact and gradient-based methods offer strong
convergence guarantees but require differentiability assumptions that
most real-world objective functions do not satisfy. Classical local
random search methods, most notably the algorithm of Solis and Wets
\citep{SolisAndWets}, instead perturb a single candidate solution
and accept improving moves, offering simplicity at the cost of a tendency
to stall in local optima. To escape this limitation, the field turned
to population-based metaheuristics, which explore the search space
with many candidate solutions simultaneously. Early and influential
examples include simulated annealing \citep{SA}, genetic algorithms
\citep{GA}, together with more recent refinements to their initialization
strategies such as k-means-based seeding \citep{GiantArmadillo},
particle swarm optimization \citep{PSO}, and the Covariance Matrix
Adaptation Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES) \citep{CMAES}, later extended
to large-scale problems via limited-memory variants such as LM-CMA-ES
\citep{LMCMAES}. More recently, a growing number of bio-inspired
metaheuristics have been proposed and subsequently refined through
hybridization with local search and modern termination criteria, as
in the case of the Giant-Armadillo Optimization method \citep{InitialDistribution}.

Among population-based metaheuristics, Differential Evolution (DE),
introduced by Storn and Price \citep{DE} and since surveyed extensively
\citep{DE2}, has proven especially effective and has spawned an active
line of successive refinements. JADE \citep{JADE} introduced the
current-to-pbest mutation strategy together with an external archive
of recently displaced solutions, both of which became standard components
of later variants. SHADE \citep{SHADE} replaced JADE's single adaptive
parameter pair with a historical memory of successful settings, and
L-SHADE \citep{LSHADE} combined this with a linear population size
reduction scheme building on earlier work on population sizing \citep{DEd}.
jSO \citep{JSO} refined this further with progress-dependent parameter
schedules, and subsequent work introduced rank-based selective pressure
in choosing difference vectors \citep{LSHADE2}, culminating in NL-SHADE-RSP
\citep{NL-SHADE-RSP}, which combined non-linear population size reduction
with rank-based selection and adaptive archiving. A parallel line
of research explored hybridizing DE with other search paradigms and
enriching its crossover operator: CoBiDE \citep{CoBiDE} introduced
covariance-guided, bimodal parameter sampling; individual-dependent
DE variants (IDE) proposed alternative generation-based scheduling
\citep{DEp}, eigenvector-based (\textquotedbl eigen\textquotedbl )
crossover, aligning the crossover operation with the population's
natural axes of variation rather than the raw coordinate axes, was
introduced in \citep{SPS-L-SHADE-EIG} and later integrated into a
cooperative multi-algorithm framework, EA4Eig, that combines CoBiDE,
IDE, CMA-ES, and jSO under a shared eigen-crossover mechanism \citep{EI4Eig}.
Separately, other general-purpose mechanisms have been proposed for
escaping stagnation and premature convergence, including opposition-based
learning \citep{obl}, \citep{obde}, Lévy-flight perturbations based
on the numerical simulation method of Mantegna \citep{Mantegna},
and, from the domain of sequential decision-making, Thompson sampling
as a principled method for adaptively allocating trials between competing
strategies \citep{Thompson}.

The method presented in this article, SPARQ, is the latest member
of a family of Differential-Evolution-based optimizers developed internally,
beginning with ARQ \citep{ARQ} and its direct successor ARQ2 \citep{ARQ2},
both of which combine a current-to-pbest DE mutation strategy with
an alternative individual-dependent evolution strategy (IDE) selected
via an adaptive roulette mechanism, restricted tournament replacement,
an external archive, and outlier quarantine. SPARQ builds directly
on ARQ2's architecture while replacing nearly every one of its fixed
or singly-adapted mechanisms with an adaptive counterpart drawn from
the DE literature surveyed above, among them a SHADE-style historical
memory in place of a single running parameter average, non-linear
population size reduction, rank-based selective pressure in the style
of NL-SHADE-RSP, a jSO-style progress-dependent schedule for its elitist
pressure, eigen-coordinate crossover, a Thompson-sampling bandit in
place of frequency-based strategy selection, and Lévy-flight-based
quarantine perturbations. On top of this synthesis, SPARQ introduces
two mechanisms with no counterpart in ARQ2 or the algorithms above:
a stagnation-gated local polishing phase based on the classical Solis-Wets
adaptive random search, and a short-term memory of recently successful
search directions (\textquotedbl trajectory echo\textquotedbl )
that is replayed during this local phase.

The remainder of this article is organized as follows. Section \ref{subsec:ARQandARQ2}
describes the architecture inherited from ARQ2 in detail. Section
\ref{sec:sparq} presents each of SPARQ's adaptive and novel mechanisms
in turn, together with the motivation behind each. Section 4 describes
the experimental configuration and reports and discusses the results,
comparing SPARQ against nine other state-of-the-art optimizers on
the standard-dimensionality benchmark and against five large-scale
optimizers on the high-dimensional benchmark, together with an analysis
of computational scalability with dimension. Section \ref{sec:conclusions}
concludes.

\section{Background: The ARQ and ARQ2 Predecessor Methods\label{subsec:ARQandARQ2}}

\subsection{ARQ: A Cohesive Optimization Design with Outlier Quarantine\label{subsec:ARQ}}

ARQ \citep{ARQ} is a differential-evolution-based optimizer built
around a single, cohesive update cycle that combines four complementary
mechanisms.

Archive-assisted pbest/1/bin trial generation. Each new candidate
is generated by attracting a target vector toward one of the top-p
fraction of the population, plus a difference term drawn partly from
the live population and partly from an external archive of recently
displaced solutions:

\[
v=x+F\,(x_{pbest}-x)+F\,(r_{1}-r_{2}),\qquad r_{1}\in P_{t},\ r_{2}\in P_{t}\cup A_{t}.
\]

This is followed by binomial crossover and projection back onto the
feasible box.

Success-history parameter adaptation. The scale factor $F$ and crossover
rate $CR$ are drawn from a Cauchy and a Normal distribution, respectively,
centered on running means $\mu_{F}$\LyXZeroWidthSpace , $\mu_{CR}$\LyXZeroWidthSpace .
After each cycle, these means are updated using gain-weighted statistics,
a weighted Lehmer mean for F and a weighted arithmetic mean for $CR$,
so that parameter settings that produced larger fitness gains are
trusted more.

Restricted Tournament Replacement (RTR). A trial vector first competes
against its own parent, if it fails to improve, it is instead compared
against its nearest neighbor (in bounds-normalized distance) within
a small random pool drawn from the population, and replaces that neighbor
if superior. This localizes selection pressure and helps preserve
population diversity.

Outlier quarantine and targeted micro-restart. ARQ's central contribution
is an event-driven mechanism that detects fitness outliers using a
robust interquartile-range threshold $\theta=Q3+a\cdot IQR$ and gently
relocates a fraction of them toward a robust center (the mean of the
best half of the population) under a small perturbation, accepting
the repair only if it improves fitness. A complementary micro-restart,
triggered after a fixed number of non-improving iterations, perturbs
a small fraction of the worst individuals around the current incumbent.
Together, these two mechanisms convert destabilizing outliers into
controlled exploration stimuli, narrowing the gap between best-case
and average-case performance without sacrificing peak results.

\subsection{ARQ2: A Roulette-Guided Hybrid Extension of ARQ\label{subsec:ARQ2}}

ARQ2 \citep{ARQ2} preserves this entire stability-oriented backbone
but extends it into an asymmetric hybrid architecture in which two
internal search branches, ARQ and an auxiliary IDE (Individual-dependent
Differential Evolution) branch, operate on a shared population and
a shared external archive.

Roulette-based branch scheduling. At each iteration, a controller
selects which of the two branches to execute, based on branch \textquotedbl credits\textquotedbl{}
that are updated in proportion to each branch's recent number of successful
replacements. Branches with higher recent credit are selected more
often, but a reset mechanism restores both branches to a common baseline
credit whenever either branch's selection probability falls below
a threshold $\delta$, preventing one branch from permanently dominating
and collapsing the hybrid into a single-strategy method. An initial
bootstrap phase enforces the ARQ branch for a fixed number of early
iterations, before branch competition becomes informative.

The IDE branch. When selected, this branch constructs its donor vectors
using its own, per-individual memories of $F$ and $CR$, and a progress-dependent
elite-guided pool whose size and guidance strength shift over the
course of the search, favoring stronger elite guidance early on, and
a more balanced elite/random mixture later. It further switches between
two donor forms (guidance-based and current-based) depending on a
phase threshold that adapts if the branch's success rate stalls.

An enriched ARQ branch. When the ARQ branch is selected, its mutation
rule is enriched with a jSO-style, progress-dependent attraction coefficient
$K(\pi)$ that modulates how strongly each new candidate is pulled
toward an elite exemplar, growing more elitist as the search progresses.

Asymmetric stabilization. Importantly, the quarantine and micro-restart
mechanisms inherited from ARQ remain attached only to the ARQ branch.
This preserves the original design's corrective role without duplicating
the same machinery inside the auxiliary IDE branch, while still letting
IDE contribute a structurally distinct search dynamic that helps on
multimodal landscapes.

Empirically, this hybridization was shown to primarily strengthen
repeated-run reliability: across 36 benchmark problems, ARQ2 improved
on ARQ's mean-value performance on 21 problems (matching it on 11,
and trailing on only 4), while remaining highly competitive in best-case
terms \citep{ARQ2}.

\section{The Proposed Method: SPARQ\label{sec:sparq}}

\subsection{The mechanisms of the SPARQ\label{subsec:mechanisms}}

SPARQ is built directly on top of ARQ2's architecture (Section \ref{subsec:ARQ2}):
it retains the shared population, the shared external archive, and
the two-branch structure (ARQ vs. IDE), but it replaces nearly every
one of ARQ2's fixed or singly-adapted mechanisms with an adaptive
counterpart, and adds several mechanisms with no counterpart in ARQ2
at all. This section describes each addition in turn.

\textbf{Population State and Non-Linear Population Size Reduction
(NLPSR)}\label{subsec:NLPSR}

Unlike ARQ2, which runs with a fixed population size $N$ throughout,
SPARQ starts from an initial size $N_{init}$ (by default scaled with
dimension, $N_{init}=18D$) and shrinks it toward a floor $N_{min}$
as the search progresses, following

\[
N(t)=\text{round}\Big(N_{init}+(N_{min}-N_{init})\cdot p(t)^{,e(t)}\Big),\qquad e(t)=1-(1-\alpha_{\text{NL}})\cdot p(t),
\]
where $p(t)\in[0,1]$ is the fraction of the evaluation budget consumed
so far and $\alpha_{\text{NL}}\in(0,1]$ controls the shape of the
reduction: $\alpha_{\text{NL}}=1$ recovers a purely linear LSHADE-style
schedule, while $\alpha_{\text{NL}}<1$produces a near-linear reduction
early in the search that accelerates as the budget is exhausted, in
the spirit of NL-SHADE-RSP. At the end of every iteration, if the
current population exceeds the target size, the worst individuals
are discarded, freeing up evaluations for the remaining ones. The
cached eigenbasis (Section \ref{subsec:EigenCrossover}) is invalidated
whenever the population shrinks, since it must be recomputed on the
new, smaller sample.

For the comparative evaluation reported in Section \ref{sec:ExperimentalResults},
however, $N_{init}$\LyXZeroWidthSpace{} was fixed at 100 for SPARQ
and for every other population-based competitor in the comparison,
overriding the dimension-scaled default $N_{init}=18D$. This choice
ensures that all compared methods start from an identical population
size rather than from method-specific, dimension-dependent values,
so that observed performance differences reflect the search mechanisms
themselves rather than differences in initial population sizing. The
dimension-scaled schedule $N_{init}=18D$ remains SPARQ's default,
recommended setting outside of this controlled comparison.

\textbf{SHADE-Style Historical Memory for F and CR}\label{subsec:3.2. SHADEStyle}

ARQ2 keeps a single, continuously-updated pair of running means ($\mu_{F},\mu_{CR}$).
SPARQ instead maintains a circular memory of size $H$, $\{(M_{F}^{k},M_{CR}^{k})\}_{k=1}^{H}$,
one slot of which (the \textquotedbl terminal\textquotedbl{} slot,
following jSO) is permanently pinned to a high value ($M_{F}^{H}=M_{CR}^{H}=0.9$)
rather than being overwritten. Before generating each trial, a memory
slot $r$ is drawn uniformly at random, and
\[
CR\sim\mathcal{N}(M_{CR}^{r},0.1),\qquad F\sim\text{Cauchy}(M_{F}^{r},0.1),
\]
both resampled/clamped to their valid ranges ($CR\in[0,1]CR\in[0,1]CR\in[0,1],F\in[F_{lo},F_{hi}]$).
Two jSO-style schedules further constrain these draws early in the
search: $CR$ is floored at 0.7 before 20\% progress and at 0.6 before
50\% progress, and $F$ is capped at $0.7+0.3\,p(t)$ before 60\%
progress. After each generation, the (non-terminal) memory slots are
updated cyclically using the same gain-weighted Lehmer/arithmetic
mean rule as ARQ2, but only one slot advances per generation rather
than the single running pair, so that a diverse set of previously-successful
settings remains available for future sampling rather than being collapsed
into one blended average.

\textbf{Rank-Based Selective Pressure and jSO-Style Elitism Schedule}\label{subsec:RankBased}

ARQ2 draws the difference-vector companions $r_{1}$ (and, when not
drawn from the archive, $r_{2}$) uniformly at random from the population.
SPARQ instead biases this choice toward better-ranked individuals:
given the population sorted by fitness (rank 0 = best), an index at
rank $r$ is selected with probability proportional to $(N-r)^{k_{r}}$\LyXZeroWidthSpace ,
where the selective-pressure exponent itself increases linearly over
the run,
\[
k_{r}(t)=k_{r,init}+(k_{r,final}-k_{r,init})\cdot p(t),\qquad k_{r,init}=2,\ k_{r,final}=3,
\]
so that rank bias is moderate early on and sharpens later, in the
style of LSHADE-RSP. Independently, the size of the $p$best pool
from which the elite-attraction target $x_{pbest}$ is drawn also
narrows over the course of the run, following a jSO-style linear schedule
from $p_{max}=0.25$ down to $p_{min}=0.10$, so that the elitist
pull becomes progressively more selective as the search matures. A
further NL-SHADE-RSP-style refinement, $CR$-sorting, reassigns the
sampled $CR$ values within each generation's batch of parents so
that better-ranked parents receive the smallest values (conservative,
fine-grained crossover) and worse-ranked parents the largest (more
disruptive recombination).

\textbf{Eigen-Coordinate Binomial Crossover}\label{subsec:EigenCrossover}

ARQ2's binomial crossover operates coordinate-by-coordinate in the
raw problem coordinates, which is ill-suited to correlated, non-separable
landscapes whose productive directions run diagonally across several
variables at once. SPARQ periodically estimates the dominant axes
of the current population's spread: using the best 50\% of individuals
(by fitness), it computes their empirical covariance matrix, diagonalizes
it via Jacobi rotation to obtain an eigenbasis $B$ and per-axis scale
factors, and refreshes this basis every $\texttt{eig\_period}$ iterations
(or whenever the population is resorted or resized). With probability
$p_{eig}=0.40$, and whenever $D$ stays within a practical cap ($D\le100$,
since the Jacobi decomposition is $\mathcal{O}(D^{3})$ per sweep),
the binomial crossover of Equation (10) in ARQ2's formulation is instead
applied in the rotated coordinate system: both the parent and the
mutant are projected onto the eigenbasis ($x_{e}=B^{\top}x$), crossover
is performed there, and the result is rotated back ($u=B\,u_{e}$).
This lets the operator recombine along the population's natural axes
of variation rather than the arbitrary problem axes.

\textbf{Thompson Sampling Bandit for Strategy Selection}\label{subsec:Thompson}

ARQ2 selects between the ARQ and IDE branches using a frequency-based
\textquotedbl credit\textquotedbl{} roulette with a threshold-triggered
reset. SPARQ replaces this with a genuine two-armed Bayesian bandit:
each branch $k$ maintains a Beta posterior with parameters ($a_{k},b_{k}$),
initialized with a mild prior favoring ARQ ($a_{ARQ}=2$) to provide
brief warm-up bias before a short deterministic bootstrap (a small
fixed number of forced ARQ iterations) hands control to the bandit.
At each iteration, a sample is drawn from each arm's posterior via
two Gamma draws, and the arm with the larger sample is selected. After
a branch executes, its posterior is updated in proportion to its observed
success rate, normalized by the number of trial evaluations actually
attempted so that a large IDE sweep and a smaller ARQ sweep are weighted
comparably, between iterations, both posteriors are geometrically
decayed toward their uninformative prior (1,1) to accommodate a non-stationary
environment, in which a strategy that was previously effective may
cease to be so as the population and progress evolve.

\textbf{Lévy-Flight Quarantine}\label{subsec:L=0000E9vyFlight}

ARQ2's quarantine mechanism relocates fitness outliers (detected via
the interquartile threshold $\theta=Q_{3}+\alpha\cdot IQR$) toward
the centroid of the top half of the population using an ordinary Gaussian
perturbation. SPARQ instead perturbs each repaired outlier using a
symmetric Lévy-stable step, generated via the Mantegna (1994) algorithm:
a step $u/|v|^{1/\beta}$ is formed from two correlated Gaussian draws
$u$, $v$ with $\beta=1.5$, producing a distribution that is mostly
small but occasionally very large, mimicking the search pattern of
animals foraging under scarce resources. Applied per coordinate and
scaled by the box range, this gives quarantined individuals a genuine
chance of relocating to a distant, unexplored region rather than merely
oscillating near the population centroid.

\textbf{On-Demand Opposition-Based Basin Escape}\label{subsec:ODOBB}

This mechanism has no counterpart in ARQ2. It monitors two independent
signals: the number of consecutive iterations without any improvement
in the incumbent best ($no\_improve\ge stag\_trigger$), and the population's
normalized spread (the average, per-coordinate, box-normalized standard
deviation across the population) falling below a small collapse ratio.
When both conditions hold simultaneously, and a cooldown counter has
elapsed, a fraction of the worst individuals are relocated to points
computed as a quasi-opposition of their current position, either the
exact reflection through the center of the box, or a point interpolated
between that reflection and the current incumbent best, and the relocation
is accepted only if it improves fitness. This directly targets premature
convergence: rather than waiting for a general-purpose restart, it
intervenes as soon as the population has visibly collapsed onto a
single region while genuinely failing to progress, forcing a look
at the opposite side of the search space.

\textbf{Stagnation-Gated Elite Polish}\label{subsec:StagnationPolish}

This is the most substantial addition with no analogue in ARQ2. Whenever
the incumbent best has failed to improve by a meaningful relative
margin for a number of iterations (this trigger tightens to a single
stagnant iteration in the closing quarter of the budget), SPARQ enters
a short, budget-capped burst (never more than a fixed fraction, by
default 12\%, of the evaluations consumed so far) of local refinement
attempts centered on the current best $x_{best}$. Each attempt draws
one of four probe modes at random:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Single-coordinate probing (round-robin over dimensions, so that every
coordinate is eventually visited even in high dimension): either a
coordinate-wise opposition ($x_{j}\to\ell_{j}+u_{j}-x_{j}$) or an
adaptive Gaussian perturbation of that one coordinate
\item Anisotropic eigen-aligned steps, drawn as a Gaussian in the (possibly
slightly stale) eigenbasis of Section 3.4, scaled per-axis by the
square root of the corresponding eigenvalue, so that steps are long
along the population's natural \textquotedbl valley\textquotedbl{}
and short across it
\item Trajectory echo (described in Section \ref{subsec:TrajectoryEcho});
\item Solis-Wets adaptive random search (described in Section \ref{SolisWets}
below, integrated as the fourth probe mode).
\end{enumerate}
Each successful probe (one that improves $best_{f}$) is injected
into the population by overwriting its current worst member, propagating
the refinement into the DE core's own mutation pool, probe-mode-specific
step sizes are adapted by a 1/5-success rule (grown on success, shrunk
on failure). The whole mechanism is protected by three independent
safety nets: a hard evaluation-budget cap, an exponential cooldown
backoff whenever an entire burst fails to find any improvement, and
an efficiency arbitration that compares the gain-per-evaluation achieved
by the polish against the gain-per-evaluation achieved by the ordinary
evolutionary loop over the same interval, forcing the polish to stand
down whenever the core DE search is demonstrably the better investment
of evaluations. A landscape-level self-selection rule additionally
imposes a long cooldown whenever several consecutive bursts produce
only negligible relative gains, so that the mechanism naturally becomes
inactive on landscapes (such as those with a distant global optimum
reachable only through large exploratory jumps) where local refinement
of the current incumbent is not productive.

\textbf{Trajectory Echo: A Memory of Recently Successful Directions}\label{subsec:TrajectoryEcho}

Also with no counterpart in ARQ2: whenever the DE core (either the
ARQ branch's restricted-tournament acceptance or the IDE branch's
greedy replacement) accepts a strictly improving trial, the accepted
displacement (trial vector minus the individual it replaced) is pushed
into a small, fixed-size ring buffer of at most eight entries. As
one of the elite-polish probe modes (Section \ref{subsec:StagnationPolish}),
a candidate step is formed as a random linear combination of the buffered
directions, normalized so that its expected magnitude stays comparable
to a single one of its ingredients regardless of how many are currently
buffered. This lets the polish phase replay an echo of movements that
have already proven productive during this specific run, of particular
value on problems where genuine improvement requires shifting several
coordinates together in a coordinated way, a pattern that isotropic
or single-coordinate probing cannot easily discover. Like the other
probe modes, this mechanism carries its own circuit breaker: a run
of repeated failures disables it for the remainder of the search rather
than continuing to spend evaluations on a direction source that has
stopped paying off.

\textbf{Solis-Wets Adaptive Local Search}\label{SolisWets}

The fourth elite-polish probe mode is a single running trajectory,
based on the classical adaptive random search method of Solis and
Wets (1981), anchored at the current incumbent. A bias vector $b$
(initialized at zero) accumulates a memory of productive directions.
Each attempt draws a random perturbation $\delta$ and first tries
$x_{best}+b+\delta$; if that fails to improve, it tries the exact
reflection $x_{best}-b-\delta$ using the same random draw before
giving up. A success in the biased direction grows $b$ toward that
direction and expands the step scale, a success only in the reflected
direction shrinks $b$ and partially reverses it; and a failure in
both directions decays $b$ toward zero and contracts the step, the
classical Solis-Wets rule for avoiding wasted wandering. Because this
probe mode only ever evolves a single trajectory from the current
incumbent, rather than recombining information from two different
population members, it cannot produce the kind of structurally incoherent
points that a population-level recombination operator could produce
on permutation-symmetric landscapes.

\textbf{Hard-Stagnation Rejuvenation}\label{subsec:HardStagnationRejuvenation}

ARQ2's single micro-restart mechanism is replaced in SPARQ by a two-tier
system. A survival trigger fires whenever the population's fitness
spread has collapsed to near-zero (a looser condition than any typical
similarity-based stopping rule), and re-seeds only a small sliver
(about 5\%) of the population uniformly at \ref{subsec:HardStagnationRejuvenation}random,
at each iteration if needed; its purpose is purely to keep the population
from appearing fully converged to an external stopping rule while
genuine progress is still possible. A hard-stagnation trigger fires
only once the incumbent has failed to improve for a much longer interval
(a configurable multiple of the basin-escape threshold of Section
\ref{subsec:ODOBB}) and the population has also collapsed, when it
fires, it re-seeds a much larger fraction of the population (by default,
all but the top 25\%) uniformly at random, while explicitly preserving
the incumbent best itself, which is never part of the population and
is therefore never at risk. An escalation mechanism further tracks
whether repeated firings of either tier keep failing to produce meaningful
gains: a persistent survival-tier failure forces a hard-stagnation
escape early, and a persistent hard-stagnation failure escalates to
a near-complete reset (keeping only a small safety fraction), preventing
a strong-but-insufficient partial restart from being immediately \textquotedbl reconquered\textquotedbl{}
by an entrenched elite on unusually deceptive landscapes. The SHADE
memory of Section \ref{subsec:3.2. SHADEStyle} is deliberately preserved
rather than reset across a rejuvenation event, since it continues
to encode useful parameter statistics for the refreshed population.

\textbf{Overall Control Flow}\label{subsec:OverallControlFlow}

Each SPARQ iteration proceeds as follows. The archive is trimmed to
its capacity; a branch (ARQ or IDE) is selected, either by the initial
bootstrap or by the Thompson bandit of Section \ref{subsec:Thompson},
and executed, with the ARQ branch additionally followed by Lévy-flight
quarantine (Section \ref{subsec:L=0000E9vyFlight}). Two independent
stagnation counters are then updated, one tracking any improvement
at all (feeding the basin-escape and rejuvenation triggers), and one
tracking only meaningful relative improvement (feeding the elite-polish
trigger, so that large populations at high dimensionality, where some
individual improves the incumbent by a vanishing amount almost every
generation, do not starve the polish mechanism of activations). The
elite polish (Section \ref{subsec:StagnationPolish}), the basin escape
(Section \ref{subsec:ODOBB}), and the rejuvenation mechanism (Section
\ref{subsec:HardStagnationRejuvenation}) are then each given the
opportunity to fire, in that order, subject to their own independent
cooldowns and triggers. The population is then shrunk toward its current
NLPSR target size if needed (Section \ref{subsec:NLPSR}), and the
bandit posteriors are decayed before the next iteration begins.

\subsection{Algorithmic Summary\label{subsec:=000391lgorithmicSummary}}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
{\footnotesize INPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $f$: objective function}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^{D}$: box-constrained search
domain, dimension D}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $N_{\mathrm{init}},\ N_{\mathrm{min}},\ \alpha_{\mathrm{NL}}$:
NLPSR: initial/minimum population size and shrink-shape exponent}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $T_{\mathrm{eval}}$: maximum number of function evaluations}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $H,\ M_{F},\ M_{CR},\ F_{lo},\ F_{hi}$: SHADE memory
size, circular memories, and F bounds}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $p_{\max},\ p_{\min}$: jSO-style pbest fraction schedule}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $k_{r}^{0},\ k_{r}^{1}$: LSHADE-RSP rank-selection
pressure schedule}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $p_{\mathrm{eig}},\ T_{\mathrm{eig}},\ \rho_{\mathrm{eig}}$:
eigen-crossover probability, refresh period, and top-fraction}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\alpha_{A}$: archive-rate coefficient}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\{(a_{k},b_{k})\}_{k=1}^{2},\ \gamma_{b},\ n_{bs}$:
Thompson bandit priors (ARQ/IDE), decay rate, bootstrap length}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\alpha_{q},\ \rho_{q},\ \beta_{L},\ \sigma_{L}$:
Levy quarantine: IQR coefficient, repair fraction, Levy exponent,
step scale}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\tau_{s},\ \varepsilon_{v},\ \rho_{b},\ \kappa_{b}$:
OBL basin escape: stagnation trigger, spread-collapse ratio, fraction,
cooldown}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\tau_{p},\ \pi_{p},\ \rho_{p},\ B_{p}$: elite polish:
trigger, late-phase threshold, burst fraction, budget cap}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $m_{r},\ \kappa_{r},\ C_{r}$: rejuvenation: stagnation
factor, keep-fraction, cooldown}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize OUTPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $x^{\ast},\ f^{\ast}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize INITIALIZATION}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Set $N\leftarrow N_{\mathrm{init}}$, initialize population
$X=\{x_{i}\}_{i=1}^{N}\subset\Omega$ and evaluate all individuals}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Set archive $A\leftarrow\varnothing$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Identify the incumbent best pair $(x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Set $f^{\mathrm{prev}}\leftarrow f^{\ast}$, $s\leftarrow0$,
$s_{p}\leftarrow0$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Initialize the SHADE memory $(M_{F},M_{CR})$ with
the terminal slot fixed at 0.9}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Initialize the bandit state $(a_{1},b_{1})\leftarrow(2,1)$,
$(a_{2},b_{2})\leftarrow(1,1)$, and the bootstrap counter $n_{bs}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Initialize the individual IDE memories $\{F_{i}^{\mathrm{IDE}},CR_{i}^{\mathrm{IDE}}\}_{i=1}^{N}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Invalidate the eigen-basis and set $e\leftarrow N$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize SPARQ main pseudocode}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 01 While $e<T_{\mathrm{eval}}$ do}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 02 \hspace{0.5cm}Trim $A$ to size $\lfloor\alpha_{A}N\rfloor$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 03 \hspace{0.5cm}If $n_{bs}>0$ then}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 04 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Select the ARQ branch;
set $n_{bs}\leftarrow n_{bs}-1$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 05 \hspace{0.5cm}Else}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 06 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Sample branch $k$
via Thompson sampling from $\{(a_{k},b_{k})\}_{k=1}^{2}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 07 \hspace{0.5cm}Endif}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 08 \hspace{0.5cm}If $k=\mathrm{IDE}$ then}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 09 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Apply the IDE branch
update (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-ide})}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 10 \hspace{0.5cm}Else}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 11 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Refresh the eigen-basis
$B$ if stale (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-eigen})}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 12 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Apply the ARQ branch
update to $X$ and $A$ (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-arq})}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 13 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Apply the Levy-flight
quarantine correction using $\alpha_{q},\rho_{q},\beta_{L},\sigma_{L}$
(\ref{alg:sparq-quarantine})}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 14 \hspace{0.5cm}Endif}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 15 \hspace{0.5cm}Update the bandit posterior $(a_{k},b_{k})$
for branch $k$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 16 \hspace{0.5cm}If $f^{\ast}<f^{\mathrm{prev}}$ then}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 17 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Set $f^{\mathrm{prev}}\leftarrow f^{\ast}$,
$s\leftarrow0$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 18 \hspace{0.5cm}Else}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 19 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Set $s\leftarrow s+1$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 20 \hspace{0.5cm}Endif}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 21 \hspace{0.5cm}Update the polish-stagnation counter
$s_{p}$ from the relative gain in $f^{\ast}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 22 \hspace{0.5cm}If $s_{p}\geq\tau_{p}$ then apply
the stagnation-gated elite polish (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-polish})}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 23 \hspace{0.5cm}If $s\geq\tau_{s}$ and the population
spread has collapsed then apply the OBL basin escape (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-obl})}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 24 \hspace{0.5cm}Apply the hard-stagnation rejuvenation
check (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-rejuv})}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 25 \hspace{0.5cm}Set $N$ to the current NLPSR target
size and shrink $X$ if needed (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-nlpsr})}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 26 \hspace{0.5cm}Decay the bandit posteriors $(a_{k},b_{k})$
toward the uniform prior}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 27 \hspace{0.5cm}Trim $A$ again to size $\lfloor\alpha_{A}N\rfloor$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 28 \hspace{0.5cm}Update the stopping state and report
the current incumbent}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 29 \hspace{0.5cm}Set $e$ equal to the current number
of function evaluations}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 30 Endwhile}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 31 Return $x^{\ast},\ f^{\ast}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize\caption{Main workflow of SPARQ\label{alg:sparq-main}}
}{\footnotesize\par}
\end{algorithm}

Concretely, each pass through Algorithm 1 maps to the following steps.
The archive is trimmed at the start of the loop (line 2). Branch selection
happens in lines 3--7: a forced ARQ warm-up while the bootstrap counter
$n_{bs}>0$ (lines 3--4), after which the Thompson bandit samples
a branch from $\{(a_{k},b_{k})\}$ (lines 5--6). The chosen branch
is then executed in lines 8--14: the IDE update (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-ide})
if $k=\mathrm{IDE}$ (line 9), or, on the ARQ side, an eigen-basis
refresh if stale (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-eigen}, line 11), the
ARQ trial generation and RTR selection (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-arq},
line 12), and the Lévy-flight quarantine correction (Algorithm (\ref{alg:sparq-quarantine},
line 13). The bandit posterior for the executed branch is updated
immediately afterward (line 15). Two independent stagnation signals
are then maintained: the ordinary counter $s$, reset on any improvement
and incremented otherwise (lines 16--20), and the meaningful-gain
counter $s_{p}$\LyXZeroWidthSpace{} (line 21), which gates the elite
polish (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-polish}) once $s_{p}\geq\tau_{p}$\LyXZeroWidthSpace{}
(line 22). The remaining stabilization mechanisms fire in sequence
and are each individually gated: the OBL basin escape (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-obl})
once $s\geq\tau_{s}$\LyXZeroWidthSpace{} and the population spread
has collapsed (line 23), and the hard-stagnation rejuvenation check
(Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-rejuv}), which evaluates its own survival/hard-stagnation
conditions internally (line 24). The population is then shrunk toward
its current NLPSR target size if needed (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-nlpsr},
line 25), the bandit posteriors are decayed toward their uninformative
prior (line 26), and the archive is trimmed a second time before the
stopping state is updated and the current incumbent reported (lines
27--28).

\begin{algorithm}[H]
{\footnotesize INPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $X=\{x_{i}\}_{i=1}^{N}$: current population}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $f,\ \Omega$: objective function and feasible domain}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $g,\ g_{\max},\ g_{t},\ \Lambda,\ \lambda_{c}$: IDE
progress index, horizon, phase threshold, patience window, low-success
counter}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\{F_{i}^{\mathrm{IDE}},CR_{i}^{\mathrm{IDE}}\}_{i=1}^{N}$:
individual IDE parameter memories}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize OUTPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Updated $X$, incumbent pair $(x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$,
updated $g_{t},\ \lambda_{c}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize IDE branch update}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 01 Advance $g$ (synced to global progress or incremented),
clamped to $g_{\max}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 02 Sort $X$ in nondecreasing objective value}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 03 Set $\varepsilon(g)\leftarrow0.1+0.9\cdot10^{5(g/g_{\max}-1)}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 04 If $g<g_{t}$ then set $S_{th}\leftarrow0$; otherwise
set $S_{th}\leftarrow0.1$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 05 For each $i=1,\ldots,N$ do}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 06 \hspace{0.5cm}Sample distinct indices $o,r_{1},r_{2},r_{3}\neq i$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 07 \hspace{0.5cm}Set $m(g)\leftarrow\max\{2,\mathrm{round}(\varepsilon(g)N)\}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 08 \hspace{0.5cm}If $g\leq g_{t}$ then with probability
$0.9\,\varepsilon(g)$ choose $r_{1}^{\star}$ from the best $m(g)$
individuals; otherwise $r_{1}^{\star}\leftarrow r_{1}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 09 \hspace{0.5cm}Else with probability $0.5$ choose
$r_{1}^{\star}$ from the best $m(g)$ individuals; otherwise $r_{1}^{\star}\leftarrow r_{1}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 10 \hspace{0.5cm}If $g>g_{t}$ and a Bernoulli($0.5$)
trial succeeds then}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 11 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Set $v_{i}\leftarrow x_{i}+F_{i}^{\mathrm{IDE}}(x_{r_{1}^{\star}}-x_{o})+F_{i}^{\mathrm{IDE}}(x_{r_{2}}-x_{r_{3}})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 12 \hspace{0.5cm}Else}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 13 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Set $v_{i}\leftarrow x_{o}+F_{i}^{\mathrm{IDE}}(x_{r_{1}^{\star}}-x_{o})+F_{i}^{\mathrm{IDE}}(x_{r_{2}}-x_{r_{3}})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 14 \hspace{0.5cm}Endif}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 15 \hspace{0.5cm}Repair $v_{i}$ to $\Omega$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 16 \hspace{0.5cm}Construct $y_{i}$ by binomial crossover
of $x_{i}$ and $v_{i}$ with rate $CR_{i}^{\mathrm{IDE}}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 17 \hspace{0.5cm}Repair $y_{i}$ to $\Omega$, evaluate
$f(y_{i})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 18 Endfor}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 19 Define the success set $I_{s}=\{i:f(y_{i})<f(x_{i})\}$
and set $SR\leftarrow|I_{s}|/N$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 20 If $g<g_{t}$ then}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 21 \hspace{0.5cm}If $SR\leq S_{th}$ then $\lambda_{c}\leftarrow\lambda_{c}+1$;
otherwise $\lambda_{c}\leftarrow0$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 22 \hspace{0.5cm}If $\lambda_{c}\geq\Lambda$ then
$g_{t}\leftarrow g$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 23 Endif}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 24 For each $i\in I_{s}$ do record the accepted step
$y_{i}-x_{i}$ (trajectory echo), replace $x_{i}\leftarrow y_{i}$,
and update $(x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$ if improved}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 25 Return the updated $X,\ (x^{\ast},f^{\ast}),\ g_{t},\ \lambda_{c}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize\caption{IDE branch update in SPARQ\label{alg:sparq-ide}}
}{\footnotesize\par}
\end{algorithm}

Each individual is updated using a progress-dependent mix of guidance
from an elite pool and random companions (lines 1--17), with the
elite-guidance probability and pool size shrinking as the search matures.
Successful trials replace their parents and feed the trajectory-echo
memory (lines 19, 24), while a separate success-rate check can advance
the branch's internal phase threshold early if progress stalls (lines
20--23).

\begin{algorithm}[H]
{\footnotesize INPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $X$: population, sorted by fitness}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $D,\ N,\ \rho_{\mathrm{eig}},\ D_{\min}$: dimension,
population size, top-fraction, minimum dimension for activation}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize OUTPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $B$ (eigenvectors), $\mathbf{s}$ (per-axis scales),
validity flag}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize Eigen-basis refresh}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 01 If $D>100$ then mark the basis invalid and return}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 02 Set $top\leftarrow\max(D+2,\mathrm{round}(\rho_{\mathrm{eig}}N))$,
clamped to $top\leq N$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 03 If $D<D_{\min}$ or $top<\max(D+2,4)$ then mark
the basis invalid and return}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 04 Compute the mean $\bar{x}$ of the top $top$ individuals}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 05 Compute the empirical covariance matrix $C$ of the
top $top$ individuals about $\bar{x}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 06 Regularize the diagonal of $C$ to avoid singularity}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 07 Diagonalize $C$ via Jacobi rotation to obtain eigenvectors
$B$ and eigenvalues $w$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 08 Set the per-axis scale $s_{j}\leftarrow\mathrm{clip}(\sqrt{w_{j}/w_{\max}},\,0.05,\,1)$
for each axis $j$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 09 Mark the basis valid and return $B,\ \mathbf{s}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize\caption{Eigen-basis refresh in SPARQ\label{alg:sparq-eigen}}
}{\footnotesize\par}
\end{algorithm}

The covariance matrix of the best half of the population is computed
and diagonalized (lines 4--7) to obtain the natural axes along which
the population is currently spread. These axes, together with per-axis
scale factors, are reused by both the ARQ crossover (Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-arq})
and the elite polish (Algorithm 6) until the next refresh.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
{\footnotesize INPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $X,\ A,\ N,\ D,\ \Omega$: population, archive, size,
dimension, domain}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $H,\ M_{F},\ M_{CR},\ F_{lo},\ F_{hi}$: SHADE memory
and F bounds}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $p_{\max},\ p_{\min},\ k_{r}^{0},\ k_{r}^{1}$: pbest
and rank-pressure schedules}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $B,\ p_{\mathrm{eig}}$: eigen-basis (Algorithm 3)
and crossover probability}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $n_{\mathrm{pool}}$: restricted-tournament pool size}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize OUTPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Updated $X,\ A,\ (M_{F},M_{CR}),\ (x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$,
ARQ success statistics}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize ARQ branch update}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 01 Sort $X$ by fitness to obtain the rank order $\mathrm{ord}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 02 If the eigen-basis is stale then refresh it (Algorithm
3)}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 03 Set $m$ parents to try (dimension-dependent agent
fraction) and draw them at random from $\mathrm{ord}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 04 For each drawn parent $t=1,\ldots,m$ sample $(F_{t},CR_{t})$
from a random slot of $(M_{F},M_{CR})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 05 If CR-sorting is enabled then reassign the sampled
$CR$ values so the best-ranked parents receive the smallest ones}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 06 For each selected parent $i$ do}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 07 \hspace{0.5cm}Draw $x_{pbest}$ uniformly from the
top $\lceil p(t)N\rceil$ individuals, $p(t)$ following the $p_{\max}\!\to\!p_{\min}$
schedule}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 08 \hspace{0.5cm}Pick $r_{1}$ by rank-biased selection
with pressure $k_{r}(t)$, forbidding $i$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 09 \hspace{0.5cm}With probability $0.5$ draw $r_{2}$
from the archive $A$; otherwise pick $r_{2}$ by rank-biased selection,
forbidding $i,r_{1}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 10 \hspace{0.5cm}Compute the jSO weight $K(F_{t})$
and form the mutant $v\leftarrow x_{i}+K(F_{t})(x_{pbest}-x_{i})+F_{t}(x_{r_{1}}-x_{r_{2}})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 11 \hspace{0.5cm}Repair $v$ to $\Omega$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 12 \hspace{0.5cm}With probability $p_{\mathrm{eig}}$
apply eigen-coordinate binomial crossover using $B$; otherwise apply
classical binomial crossover with rate $CR_{t}$ to obtain the trial
$u$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 13 \hspace{0.5cm}Repair $u$ to $\Omega$ and evaluate
$f(u)$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 14 \hspace{0.5cm}If $f(u)<f(x_{i})$ then}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 15 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Archive $x_{i}$, set
$x_{i}\leftarrow u$, and record the success $(F_{t},CR_{t},\mathrm{gain})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 16 \hspace{0.5cm}Else}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 17 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Draw a pool $Q\subset X$
of size $n_{\mathrm{pool}}$ (excluding $i$) and find the nearest
$q^{\star}\in Q$ to $u$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 18 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}If $f(u)<f(q^{\star})$
then archive $q^{\star}$, set $q^{\star}\leftarrow u$, inherit its
IDE parameters from $i$, and record the success}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 19 \hspace{0.5cm}Endif}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 20 \hspace{0.5cm}Update $(x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$ if improved}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 21 Endfor}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 22 Update the memory slot of $(M_{F},M_{CR})$ from
the recorded successes}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 23 Return the updated $X,\ A,\ (M_{F},M_{CR}),\ (x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize\caption{ARQ branch trial generation and RTR selection in SPARQ\label{alg:sparq-arq}}
}{\footnotesize\par}
\end{algorithm}

For each selected parent, a mutant is formed by pulling it toward
a rank-biased elite exemplar plus a rank-biased difference term (lines
7--10), then crossed over either in the eigen-basis or in the raw
coordinates (line 11). The trial first competes with its own parent
and, failing that, with its nearest neighbor in a small random pool
(lines 13--19), with all successes feeding the SHADE memory update
(line 22).

\begin{algorithm}[H]
{\footnotesize INPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $X,\ N,\ D,\ \Omega$: population, size, dimension,
domain}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\alpha_{q},\ \rho_{q},\ \beta_{L},\ \sigma_{L}$:
IQR coefficient, repair fraction, Levy exponent, step scale}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize OUTPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Updated $X,\ A,\ (x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize Levy-flight quarantine}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 01 Compute $Q_{1},Q_{3}$ and $IQR=Q_{3}-Q_{1}$ of
the fitness values}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 02 Set the threshold $\theta\leftarrow Q_{3}+\alpha_{q}\cdot IQR$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 03 Compute the centroid $c$ of the best half of $X$
by fitness}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 04 Set the outlier set $O\leftarrow\{i:f(x_{i})\geq\theta\}$;
if $O=\varnothing$ then return}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 05 Draw a random subset $O_{\rho}\subset O$ with $|O_{\rho}|=\lfloor\rho_{q}|O|\rfloor$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 06 For each $i\in O_{\rho}$ do}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 07 \hspace{0.5cm}For each coordinate $j$ draw a Levy
step $\eta_{j}$ via the Mantegna algorithm with exponent $\beta_{L}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 08 \hspace{0.5cm}Set $x_{i}'\leftarrow c+\sigma_{L}\cdot(\mathbf{u}-\mathbf{\ell})\odot\boldsymbol{\eta}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 09 \hspace{0.5cm}Repair $x_{i}'$ to $\Omega$ and
evaluate $f(x_{i}')$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 10 \hspace{0.5cm}If $f(x_{i}')<f(x_{i})$ then archive
$x_{i}$, set $x_{i}\leftarrow x_{i}'$, and re-seed its IDE parameters}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 11 \hspace{0.5cm}Update $(x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$ if improved}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 12 Endfor}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 13 Return the updated $X,\ A,\ (x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize\caption{Levy-flight quarantine in SPARQ\label{alg:sparq-quarantine}}
}{\footnotesize\par}
\end{algorithm}

Individuals whose fitness exceeds a robust outlier threshold (line
2) are relocated toward the population's best-half centroid using
a heavy-tailed Lévy step rather than an ordinary Gaussian one (lines
7--8), giving them an occasional large jump to a genuinely different
region. A relocation is only kept if it actually improves on the original
individual (line 9).

\begin{algorithm}[H]
{\footnotesize INPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $x^{\ast},\ f^{\ast},\ X,\ N,\ D,\ \Omega$: incumbent,
population, size, dimension, domain}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $B,\ \mathbf{s}$: eigen-basis and per-axis scales
(Algorithm 3)}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\rho_{p},\ B_{p}$: burst-size fraction and evaluation-budget
cap}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\sigma_{p},\ \sigma_{c},\ b_{sw},\ \rho_{sw},\ \sigma_{e}$:
eigen-step, coordinate-step, Solis-Wets bias/step, echo scale}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize OUTPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Updated $x^{\ast},\ f^{\ast},\ X$, adapted step sizes
and cooldown state}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize Stagnation-gated elite polish}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 01 Set $f_{\mathrm{before}}\leftarrow f^{\ast}$ and
compute the evolutionary gain-per-evaluation since the last activation}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 02 Set the burst size $k\leftarrow\max(6,\mathrm{round}(\rho_{p}N))$,
enlarged late in the run to cover $D$ coordinates}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 03 Set $\mathrm{fails}\leftarrow0$, $\mathrm{wins}\leftarrow0$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 04 For $t=1,\ldots,k$ do}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 05 \hspace{0.5cm}If the evaluation budget or the cap
$B_{p}$ is exceeded then break}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 06 \hspace{0.5cm}Draw a probe mode $m\sim U(0,1)$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 07 \hspace{0.5cm}If $m<0.30$ then probe a single round-robin
coordinate (opposition or adaptive Gaussian step)}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 08 \hspace{0.5cm}Else if $m<0.65$ and $B$ is available
then draw an anisotropic Gaussian step in the eigen-basis, scaled
by $\mathbf{s}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 09 \hspace{0.5cm}Else if $m<0.80$ and the echo buffer
is non-empty then draw a random linear combination of the buffered
directions (trajectory echo)}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 10 \hspace{0.5cm}Else propose $x^{\ast}+b_{sw}+\delta$,
and if it fails, the reflection $x^{\ast}-b_{sw}-\delta$ (Solis-Wets)}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 11 \hspace{0.5cm}Repair the candidate to $\Omega$
and evaluate its fitness}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 12 \hspace{0.5cm}If the candidate improves $f^{\ast}$
then}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 13 \hspace{0.5cm}\hspace{0.5cm}Update $(x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$,
grow the step size of the active probe mode, replace the population's
worst individual, reset $\mathrm{fails}$, increment $\mathrm{wins}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 14 \hspace{0.5cm}Else shrink the step size of the active
probe mode and increment $\mathrm{fails}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 15 \hspace{0.5cm}If $\mathrm{fails}\geq10$ then break}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 16 Endfor}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 17 If $\mathrm{wins}=0$ then double the cooldown backoff;
otherwise reset it}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 18 If the polish's gain-per-evaluation is below the
evolutionary loop's then extend the cooldown}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 19 If the relative gain over the burst is negligible
for several consecutive activations then extend the cooldown further}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 20 Return the updated $x^{\ast},\ f^{\ast},\ X$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize\caption{Stagnation-gated elite polish in SPARQ\label{alg:sparq-polish}}
}{\footnotesize\par}
\end{algorithm}

While the incumbent best is stagnating, a short burst of local probes
is applied directly to it, drawn from four alternative modes, coordinate-wise,
eigen-aligned, trajectory-echo, and Solis-Wets (lines 6--10), and
any improvement is written back into the population's worst slot (line
12). Three independent safeguards (lines 16--18) shut the mechanism
down whenever it proves less productive than ordinary evolution.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
{\footnotesize INPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $X,\ x^{\ast},\ N,\ D,\ \Omega$: population, incumbent,
size, dimension, domain}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\tau_{s},\ \varepsilon_{v},\ \rho_{b},\ \kappa_{b},\ s$:
stagnation trigger, spread-collapse ratio, escape fraction, cooldown,
stagnation counter}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize OUTPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Updated $X,\ (x^{\ast},f^{\ast}),\ s$, cooldown state}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize OBL basin escape}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 01 If the cooldown counter is positive then decrement
it and return}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 02 If not ($s\geq\tau_{s}$ and the normalized population
spread $<\varepsilon_{v}$) then return}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 03 Sort the population by fitness}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 04 Set $count\leftarrow\max(1,\lfloor\rho_{b}N\rfloor)$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 05 For each of the $count$ worst individuals $x_{i}$
do}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 06 \hspace{0.5cm}For each coordinate $j$ compute the
opposite point $\ell_{j}+u_{j}-x_{i,j}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 07 \hspace{0.5cm}With probability $0.5$ use the pure
opposition; otherwise interpolate between the opposition and $x^{\ast}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 08 \hspace{0.5cm}Repair the candidate to $\Omega$
and evaluate its fitness}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 09 \hspace{0.5cm}If it improves $x_{i}$ then archive
$x_{i}$, replace it, and re-seed its IDE parameters}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 10 \hspace{0.5cm}Update $(x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$ if improved}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 11 Endfor}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 12 If any replacement was applied then reset the cooldown
to $\kappa_{b}$ and set $s\leftarrow0$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 13 Return the updated $X,\ (x^{\ast},f^{\ast}),\ s$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize\caption{On-demand OBL basin escape in SPARQ\label{alg:sparq-obl}}
}{\footnotesize\par}
\end{algorithm}

This only fires when the search has stopped improving and the population
has visibly collapsed onto one region (line 2). A fraction of the
worst individuals are then reflected to the opposite side of the search
domain (lines 6--7), and the stagnation counter is reset if this
yields any actual improvement (line 10).

\begin{algorithm}[H]
{\footnotesize INPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $X,\ x^{\ast},\ N,\ D,\ \Omega$: population, incumbent,
size, dimension, domain}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $\tau_{s},\ m_{r},\ \kappa_{r},\ C_{r},\ s$: stagnation
trigger, rejuvenation factor, keep-fraction, cooldown, stagnation
counter}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize OUTPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Updated $X,\ (x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$, cooldown and escalation
state}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize Hard-stagnation rejuvenation}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 01 If the cooldown counter is positive then decrement
it and return}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 02 Compute the relative fitness spread $f_{\mathrm{rel}}$
across the population}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 03 Set $\mathrm{collapsed}\leftarrow(f_{\mathrm{rel}}<\varepsilon_{1})\ \text{or}\ (\mathrm{spread}<\varepsilon_{2})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 04 Set $\mathrm{hard\_stag}\leftarrow(s\geq m_{r}\tau_{s})$
and (progress within its cutoff) and (spread bounded)}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 05 If $\mathrm{collapsed}$ and not $\mathrm{hard\_stag}$
then update the weak-reseed streak; escalate to $\mathrm{hard\_stag}$
once the streak exceeds its limit}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 06 Else reset the weak-reseed streak}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 07 Set $\mathrm{survival}\leftarrow\mathrm{collapsed}$
and not $\mathrm{hard\_stag}$; if neither holds then return}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 08 If $\mathrm{hard\_stag}$ then update the strong-reseed
failure streak; escalate to a near-full reset once it exceeds its
limit}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 09 Sort the population by fitness}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 10 Set the elite fraction $\mathrm{keep}$ to preserve:
a tiny sliver under a near-full reset, $\kappa_{r}$ under $\mathrm{hard\_stag}$,
or nearly all of $N$ under $\mathrm{survival}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 11 For each individual ranked below $\mathrm{keep}$
do}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 12 \hspace{0.5cm}Draw a uniformly random point in $\Omega$;
archive the old individual and replace it; re-seed its IDE parameters}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 13 \hspace{0.5cm}Update $(x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$ if improved}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 14 Endfor}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 15 If $\mathrm{hard\_stag}$ then reset the stagnation
counter $s$ and set a long cooldown; otherwise set a short cooldown}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 16 Return the updated $X,\ (x^{\ast},f^{\ast})$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize\caption{Hard-stagnation rejuvenation in SPARQ\label{alg:sparq-rejuv}}
}{\footnotesize\par}
\end{algorithm}

A loose \textquotedbl survival\textquotedbl{} condition keeps re-seeding
a small sliver of the population indefinitely to avoid the appearance
of full convergence, while a much stricter \textquotedbl hard-stagnation\textquotedbl{}
condition, reached only after prolonged non-improvement, re-seeds
the large majority of the population (lines 2--10). Two escalation
counters (lines 5, 8) force a stronger response whenever repeated
firings of either condition keep failing to produce real gains.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
{\footnotesize INPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $X,\ N,\ N_{\mathrm{init}},\ N_{\min},\ \alpha_{\mathrm{NL}}$:
population, current size, size bounds, shrink-shape exponent}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - $p$: current progress ratio}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize OUTPUT}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize - Updated $X$ and $N$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize NLPSR population-size reduction}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 01 Compute the shrink-shape exponent $q\leftarrow1-(1-\alpha_{\mathrm{NL}})p$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 02 Compute the target size $N_{\mathrm{target}}\leftarrow\mathrm{round}(N_{\mathrm{init}}+(N_{\min}-N_{\mathrm{init}})p^{q})$,
clamped to $[N_{\min},N_{\mathrm{init}}]$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 03 If $N_{\mathrm{target}}\geq N$ then return unchanged}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 04 Sort the population ascending by fitness}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 05 Keep the best $N_{\mathrm{target}}$ individuals
(and their IDE memories); discard the rest}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 06 Invalidate the eigen-basis and set $N\leftarrow N_{\mathrm{target}}$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize 07 Return the updated $X,\ N$}{\footnotesize\par}

{\footnotesize\caption{NLPSR population-size reduction in SPARQ\label{alg:sparq-nlpsr}}
}{\footnotesize\par}
\end{algorithm}

The overall control flow of SPARQ, summarizing the sequence of decisions
executed within each iteration of the main loop, is illustrated in
Figure \ref{fig:floatChart}. The diagram highlights the probabilistic
selection between the ARQ and IDE branches, the mandatory Lévy-flight
quarantine attached to the ARQ branch, and the three graduated stagnation-driven
mechanisms, namely the stagnation-gated elite polish, the opposition-based
basin escape, and the hard-stagnation rejuvenation, together with
their respective activation gates.

\begin{figure}[H]
\begin{centering}
\includegraphics[scale=0.6]{flowchart}
\par\end{centering}
\caption{Overall control flow of the SPARQ algorithm, illustrating branch selection,
the ARQ-attached quarantine mechanism, and the graduated stagnation-driven
activation gates\label{fig:floatChart}}

\end{figure}

The algorithm Figure \ref{fig:floatChart} operates within a loop
governed exclusively by the evaluation budget ($e<T_{eval}$). At
the start of every pass through this loop, the external archive is
first trimmed to size $\lfloor\alpha_{A}\cdot N\rfloor$, preventing
it from growing unchecked as iterations accumulate.

The first substantive decision the system makes concerns which of
the two search branches, ARQ or IDE, will be executed. As long as
the bootstrap counter remains positive, the ARQ branch is enforced
outright, with no genuine choice involved, guaranteeing a brief warm-up
period before the bandit's statistics become reliable. Once this period
elapses, branch selection passes to a Thompson-sampling bandit, which
draws a random sample from each branch's posterior distribution and
favors whichever branch returns the larger value. This selection is
not a hard on/off switch but a probabilistic one: a branch that performs
consistently worse will be chosen less often, yet it is never permanently
excluded, since both posteriors decay toward the uninformative prior
at every iteration, allowing a branch that was previously effective
to be revisited later.

Depending on the outcome of this choice, execution diverges considerably.
If the IDE branch is selected, only its own update is executed, with
no accompanying mechanism. If the ARQ branch is selected, the eigen-basis
is first checked for staleness and refreshed if necessary, the ARQ
update itself is then executed, and the Lévy-flight quarantine correction
is applied immediately afterward as a mandatory step. This quarantine
mechanism is structurally attached exclusively to the ARQ branch and
is never triggered when IDE executes, regardless of how often the
latter is chosen. Both paths, however, converge on the same subsequent
step: updating the bandit's posterior for whichever branch was just
executed.

Two independent stagnation counters are then updated, each feeding
a different downstream mechanism. The first records any absence of
improvement whatsoever, however small, and feeds the basin-escape
and rejuvenation mechanisms. The second records only the absence of
meaningful, relative improvement, and feeds the elite-polish mechanism
exclusively. This separation is deliberate: in large populations or
at high dimensionality, some individual improves the incumbent by
a negligible amount in nearly every generation, so a shared counter
would almost never allow the polish phase to activate.

Three activation gates follow, always evaluated in the same order
within a given iteration, and not mutually exclusive. The first concerns
the elite polish, which is triggered once the second counter exceeds
its stagnation threshold; once activated, it runs a short burst of
local probes, alternating among single-coordinate probing, anisotropic
eigen-aligned steps, replay from the trajectory-echo memory, and the
classical Solis-Wets adaptive search, all protected by three independent
safeguards: a hard evaluation-budget cap, an exponential cooldown
whenever an entire burst fails to yield any improvement, and a gain-per-evaluation
comparison against the ordinary evolutionary loop, which forces the
polish to stand down whenever the core DE search proves the better
use of the same evaluations. The second gate concerns the opposition-based
basin-escape mechanism, which fires only when prolonged non-improvement
and a visible collapse in population spread occur simultaneously,
and only once its own cooldown has elapsed; if it produces no improvement,
the cooldown is renewed without resetting the stagnation counter.
The third gate, hard-stagnation rejuvenation, is not a simple binary
decision but a hierarchical one: it internally evaluates whether only
the milder survival condition holds, re-seeding a small fraction of
the population at random without materially disrupting progress, or
whether the stricter hard-stagnation condition holds, re-seeding the
large majority of the population while preserving only its elite members.
It further maintains its own escalation counters, so that repeated
failures of the mild reseeding force an earlier transition to the
harder condition, and repeated failures of the harder condition escalate
to a near-complete reset.

Two further mechanisms execute at every iteration with no gating condition
at all: population-size reduction via NLPSR, and the decay of the
bandit's posteriors toward the uniform prior. NLPSR is always invoked,
but effectively does nothing whenever the current target size is not
smaller than the current population, so it remains continuously active
without always being consequential. The iteration closes with a second
trimming of the archive, since the population size may have changed,
and with an update to the current incumbent best, before the loop
returns to its head until the evaluation budget is exhausted and the
final solution is returned.

The overall picture that emerges is one of two permanently available
search branches, whose relative usage is regulated probabilistically
rather than through hard activation or deactivation, surrounded by
an increasingly rare and increasingly disruptive layer of rescue mechanisms,
each gated by strictly graduated stagnation conditions and each equipped
with its own safeguards that switch it off automatically once it proves
ineffective.

\section{Experimental results\label{sec:ExperimentalResults}}

\subsection{Experimental Configuration\label{subsec:generalsettings}}

All methods evaluated in this study, the proposed SPARQ algorithm
together with the thirteen competing optimizers, were implemented
in ANSI C++ within the open-source OptimSolution \citep{optimsolution}
framework, developed by Vasileios Charilogis and publicly available
at \url{https://github.com/charilog/optimsolution} (accessed 18 July
2026). All experiments were compiled and executed under Debian 12.12
with GCC 13.4, on a workstation equipped with an AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D
processor (16 cores, 32 threads) and 64 GB of DDR4 memory. To account
for the stochastic nature of the algorithms, each benchmark problem
was solved over 30 independent runs, every run initialized with a
distinct random seed. Across all runs and all methods, the evaluation
budget was fixed at 150,000 function evaluations, consistent with
the termination criterion adopted by the CEC 2011 real-world optimization
benchmark \citep{de_CEC2011} and allowing direct comparison with
prior work. The best and mean objective values obtained at termination,
averaged over the 30 independent runs per problem, are reported as
the primary performance measures throughout the results section. Table
\ref{tab:settings} summarizes the SPARQ parameter settings actually
used in these experiments, expressed in the notation introduced in
Algorithm $\ref{alg:sparq-main}$ and Sections $\ref{subsec:NLPSR}$--$\ref{subsec:HardStagnationRejuvenation}$,
while Table \ref{tab:constants} lists the internal constants that
remain fixed at compile time and are therefore not exposed through
the configuration interface.
\begin{flushleft}
{\scriptsize{}%
\begin{longtable}[c]{lll}
\caption{SPARQ parameter settings used in the experiments, following the notation
introduced in Algorithm \ref{alg:sparq-main} and Sections \ref{subsec:NLPSR}--\ref{subsec:HardStagnationRejuvenation}\label{tab:settings}}
\tabularnewline
\toprule 
{\scriptsize\textbf{Symbol}} & {\scriptsize\textbf{Value}} & {\scriptsize\textbf{Description}}\tabularnewline
\midrule
\endhead
\midrule 
\multicolumn{3}{l}{{\scriptsize General configuration}}\tabularnewline
{\scriptsize$N_{fe}$} & {\scriptsize 150,000} & {\scriptsize Evaluation budget per run}\tabularnewline
{\scriptsize Runs} & {\scriptsize 30} & {\scriptsize Independent repeated runs}\tabularnewline
{\scriptsize Initialization} & {\scriptsize uniform} & {\scriptsize Population initialization distribution}\tabularnewline

\subsection{Comparative Results on Standard-Dimensionality Benchmark Problems}

This subsection presents the outcome of the comparative evaluation
on the standard-dimensionality portion of the benchmark suite. SPARQ
is compared against nine state-of-the-art optimizers: ARQ2 \citep{ARQ2},
EA4Eig \citep{EI4Eig}, jSO \citep{JSO}, LM-CMA-ES \citep{LMCMAES},
mLSHADE-RL \citep{mLSHADE-RL}, NL-SHADE-LBC \citep{NL-SHADE-LBC},
SFCDE \citep{SFCDE}, TridentDE \citep{TridentDE}, and UDE3 \citep{UDE3}.
The comparison covers forty-five benchmark problems. These include
classical synthetic test functions alongside real-world engineering
formulations, such as antenna array design, hydrothermal scheduling,
economic load dispatch, and several chemical-engineering problems.
Problem dimensionality in this subsection ranges from as few as five
variables to several hundred. For each problem, every method was executed
independently over a fixed number of repeated runs under an identical
evaluation budget. Three complementary tables report the results.
Table \ref{tab:comparison1} presents detailed statistics for each
method and problem, namely the best value found, the mean value, the
success rate, the standard deviation, and the execution time. Table
\ref{tab:bestRankin1} reports the ranking of all ten methods on each
problem according to the best value achieved. Table \ref{tab:meanRanking1}
reports the corresponding ranking according to the mean value, offering
a stricter measure of reliability across repeated runs. Together,
these three tables allow the performance of SPARQ to be assessed both
in absolute terms and relative to its competitors, and both in terms
of peak performance and average-case consistency.

Table \ref{tab:comparison1} reports Best, Mean, Rate\%, SD, and execution
time for all ten methods on each of the forty-five problems. Aggregated
across the full set, SPARQ attains a success rate of 100\% on 15 problems,
or 33.3\% of the total. Its average success rate is 46.6\%, the highest
figure among the ten methods. The next-best average success rates
are 31.4\% for mLSHADE-RL, 30.4\% for EA4Eig, and 27.6\% for UDE3.
ARQ2, LM-CMA-ES, SFCDE, TridentDE, and NL-SHADE-RSP each average below
25\%. The magnitude of this advantage is clearly visible on individual
problems. On alpine1 (d=100), bucherastrigin (d=50), salomon (d=100),
and weierstrass (d=70), SPARQ and LM-CMA-ES jointly reach a mean error
of 0 at 100\% success. The remaining eight methods are confined to
3 to 7\% success. Their mean errors on bucherastrigin, for instance,
range from 18.46 for EA4Eig to 87.45 for TridentDE. On powell (d=100)
and sinusoidal (d=200), SPARQ alone reaches 100\% success, with a
mean error of 0 and -3.5 respectively. Every other method is held
to 3 to 20\% success on these two problems. LM-CMA-ES in particular
sees its sinusoidal mean collapse to -1.02. On the real-world load-dispatch
and engineering problems, namely eld2 through eld5, hydrothermal,
tersoffb, tersoffc, and transmissionpricing, every method in the comparison
records success rates no higher than 3 to 10\%. SPARQ is no exception
here. This pattern indicates that the difficulty is intrinsic to these
landscapes rather than a shortcoming of any single method. In terms
of computational cost, SPARQ's cumulative execution time across all
forty-five problems is 22.04 seconds. This is the second-lowest total
among the ten methods, behind only LM-CMA-ES at 13.47 seconds. ARQ2
requires 60.95 seconds, SFCDE 78.01 seconds, EA4Eig 102.45 seconds,
UDE3 157.60 seconds, and TridentDE 254.43 seconds. TridentDE alone
requires roughly eleven times SPARQ's total runtime for the same benchmark
set.

Table \ref{tab:bestRankin1} reports the ranking of each method according
to its best-value result. It covers the same ten methods and forty-five
problems. SPARQ attains rank 1, whether shared or exclusive, on 25
of the 45 problems. This corresponds to 55.6\%, the highest share
among all ten methods. If only cases where SPARQ is the sole method
at rank 1 are counted, with no ties, this figure falls to 4 of 45,
or 8.9\%. By comparison, jSO achieves 5 of 45 exclusive first places,
and ARQ2 achieves 3 of 45. This indicates that a substantial part
of SPARQ's rank-1 share arises from problems where several methods
jointly reach the global optimum, rather than from unique dominance.
The mean rank across all forty-five problems is 2.64 for SPARQ. This
is the lowest, and therefore best, mean rank of the ten methods. It
sits ahead of jSO at 2.80 and ARQ2 at 2.91. At the other extreme,
LM-CMA-ES and NL-SHADE-RSP average 6.38 and 6.29 respectively. They
register the worst rank, 10th, on 20 problems (44.4\%) and 11 problems
(24.4\%) respectively.

Table \ref{tab:meanRanking1}reports the corresponding ranking based
on mean value. This is a stricter criterion, since it rewards consistency
across repeated runs rather than a single strong result. Under this
criterion, SPARQ ranks first on 20 of 45 problems, or 44.4\%. This
is a lower share than the 55.6\% obtained under the best-value criterion.
Its exclusive, untied first-place count rises slightly to 6 of 45,
compared with 7 of 45 for jSO. Despite this lower first-place frequency,
SPARQ's mean rank across the benchmark set is 3.02. This is marginally
better than EA4Eig at 3.04, and it is the best mean rank among all
ten methods. SPARQ never registers the worst rank, 10th, on any problem.
The divergence between the best-value and mean-value criteria is most
pronounced on rotatedrosenbrock. There, SPARQ's rank worsens from
5th to 8th. On tersoffc it worsens from 7th to 8th. On potential,
by contrast, it improves from 2nd to 1st.

\subsection{Comparative Results on Large-Scale, High-Dimensional Benchmark Problems}

This subsection presents the outcome of the comparative evaluation
on the large-scale portion of the benchmark suite. SPARQ is compared
against five optimizers developed specifically for high-dimensional
search: CCDG2 \citep{CCDG2_1,CCDG2_2}, LM-CMA-ES \citep{LMCMAES},
MMES \citep{MMES}, RMES \citep{RMES}, and VkD-CMA-ES \citep{VkD-CMA-ESc}.
The comparison covers seventeen benchmark problems, each evaluated
at two dimensions, d = 1000 and d = 10,000, for a total of thirty-four
problem-dimension combinations. The problem set spans separable functions
of low intrinsic difficulty alongside strongly non-separable and multimodal
landscapes, allowing both the accuracy and the computational scalability
of each method to be assessed as the number of variables grows by
an order of magnitude. As in the previous subsection, every method
was executed independently over a fixed number of repeated runs under
an identical evaluation budget. Three complementary tables report
the results. Table \ref{tab:comparison2} presents detailed statistics
for each method and problem-dimension combination, namely the best
value found, the mean value, the success rate, the standard deviation,
and the execution time. Table \ref{tab:bestRankin2} reports the ranking
of all six methods according to the best value achieved, and Table
\ref{tab:meanRanking2} reports the corresponding ranking according
to the mean value.
